GitHub
● Niagara · Custom HLSL · GPU Particle Simulation

Niagara HLSL Reference

Engine: UE 5.x
Context: Scratch Pad · Stateless Modules
Stage: Spawn · Update · Render
⚗ HLSL Reference
Scratch Pad Module HLSL
Scratch Pad Basics
✓Niagara uses a restricted HLSL subset — not the same as Material Custom nodes
✓In the Scratch Pad UI, add input/output map pins and bind each to a Particles.X attribute — the local pin name is what you use in HLSL
✓Parameters.X refers to module-level user parameters (exposed inputs), NOT particle attributes
✓ExecutionIndex gives the current particle index for per-particle logic
✓Use Emitter.LocalSpace / Emitter.Age for emitter-level data
✓Write results via your output pin names — no return statement, no OutputParameters
Scratch Pad Access Pattern
// Bind pins in Scratch Pad UI: Particles.Position → "InPosition"
// In HLSL you use the LOCAL PIN NAME you chose in the UI
float3 Pos      = InPosition;   // bound to Particles.Position
float  Age      = InAge;        // bound to Particles.Age
float  Life     = InLifetime;   // bound to Particles.Lifetime
float  DT       = InDeltaTime;  // bound to Engine.DeltaTime
int    ID       = InUniqueID;   // bound to Particles.UniqueID

// Writing: assign to your output pin names (bound in UI)
OutPosition = Pos + InVelocity * DT; // → Particles.Position
OutColor    = float4(1,0,0,1);        // → Particles.Color

// No return, no OutputParameters — output pins are the writes
Niagara Random Intrinsics

Niagara provides seeded random functions (not HLSL standard):

RandomUInt(seed)Seeded random integer — deterministic per particle
RandomFloat(seed)Seeded random float [0,1]
RandomUnitFloat3(seed)Random direction on unit sphere
Particles.UniqueIDBind to an input pin; use as hash seed for per-particle randomness
Restrictions & Things to Avoid
Niagara HLSL · Never Do This
✗No raw Texture2DSample() calls in Scratch Pad HLSL — use the Texture Sample Data Interface module instead; it wraps the texture correctly for GPU sim
✗No dynamic loop bounds — use [unroll] or fixed iteration counts
✗No ddx / ddy — these are pixel shader only; Niagara sim runs in compute
✗No return statements in Scratch Pad — write to output parameters instead
✗No struct definitions in Scratch Pad — use flat parameter lists
✗Don't assume Material intrinsics — many UE material functions aren't available
✗No UAV writes in standard modules — data flows through typed SRV buffers only
Simulation vs Render Stage
!Simulation HLSL (Spawn/Update) runs as compute — no raw texture calls in HLSL, no derivatives; use Texture Sample Data Interface for texture access
!Render HLSL (Particle Render) differs from simulation HLSL — different available functions
!Data Interface HLSL runs in its own context — check DI docs for available functions
✓Branching is allowed — use BRANCH hint for dynamic branching, FLATTEN to evaluate both
✓Switch statements work in Niagara HLSL — useful for loop mode selection
What IS Available
✓Standard math: lerp, saturate, smoothstep, clamp, abs, sign, pow
✓Vector math: dot, cross, normalize, length, distance
✓Trig: sin, cos, tan, atan2, asin, acos
✓Floor/ceil: floor, ceil, round, frac, fmod
✓Flow control: BRANCH, FLATTEN, UNROLL, LOOP hints
✓Typed buffer reads: Buffer<float> SRV access via index
Stateless Module Patterns · From Real Code
Stateless Particle Struct

Stateless modules use FStatelessParticle + FStatelessParticleContext — different from Scratch Pad:

// Stateless module entry point
void MyModule_Initialize(
    inout FStatelessParticle Particle,
    in FStatelessParticleContext Context)
{
  // Read state
  uint   Idx = Context.ExecIndex;
  float3 Pos = Particle.Position;
  float  Age = Particle.Age;

  // Write state directly
  Particle.Position         = NewPos;
  Particle.PreviousPosition = OldPos;
  Particle.Scale            = float3(1,1,1);
  Particle.Color            = float4(1,0,0,1);
  Particle.MeshOrientation  = NewQuat;
}
GPU Buffer Access Pattern

Stateless modules use typed Buffer<float> SRVs — read 3 or 4 consecutive floats per element:

// Reading float3 from typed float buffer
float3 ReadFloat3FromBuffer(
    Buffer<float> InBuffer, uint Index)
{
  uint Base = Index * 3;
  return float3(
    InBuffer[Base + 0],
    InBuffer[Base + 1],
    InBuffer[Base + 2]);
}

// Reading float4 (color, quaternion)
float4 ReadFloat4FromBuffer(
    Buffer<float> InBuffer, uint Index)
{
  uint Base = Index * 4;
  return float4(
    InBuffer[Base + 0], InBuffer[Base + 1],
    InBuffer[Base + 2], InBuffer[Base + 3]);
}
Indexing Strategies

Common patterns for selecting which array element a particle uses:

// Sequential (round-robin by particle ID)
uint Idx = ParticleID % NumPositions;

// Sequential with clamping (no wrap)
uint Idx = min(ParticleID, NumPositions - 1);

// Random via MurmurHash3 finalizer
uint Hash = ParticleID;
Hash ^= Hash >> 16;
Hash *= 0x85ebca6b;
Hash ^= Hash >> 13;
Hash *= 0xc2b2ae35;
Hash ^= Hash >> 16;
uint Idx = Hash % NumPositions;

// Age-based (interpolate through array)
float T   = NormalizedAge * (float)Count;
uint  Idx = min((uint)T, Count - 1);
Particle Attribute Reference
Particle Attributes · Scratch Pad vs Stateless Access
AttributeNiagara NamespaceStateless StructNotes
**Position**Particles.PositionParticle.PositionBind to Scratch Pad pin; world or local-space per emitter setting
**Previous Position**Particles.PreviousPositionParticle.PreviousPositionLast frame's position — for velocity/trail computation
**Velocity**Particles.VelocityDerived from Pos deltaScratch Pad has explicit velocity; stateless derives it
**Age**Particles.AgeParticle.AgeTime since particle spawned (seconds)
**Lifetime**Particles.LifetimeParticle.LifetimeTotal lifespan (seconds)
**Normalized Age**Particles.Age / Particles.LifetimeParticle.Age / Particle.Lifetime0→1 over lifetime; compute manually in both contexts
**Color**Particles.ColorParticle.Colorfloat4 RGBA
**Scale**Particles.ScaleParticle.Scalefloat3 per-axis scale
**Mesh Orientation**Particles.MeshOrientationParticle.MeshOrientationfloat4 quaternion
**Execution Index**ExecutionIndexContext.ExecIndexPer-particle index for deterministic behavior
**Unique ID**Particles.UniqueIDContext.ParticleIDPersistent ID — use as hash seed for randomness
**Delta Time**Engine.DeltaTimeNot directly availableFrame delta in seconds — bind via Engine namespace
Patterns & Tips
Useful Patterns
✓Random per-particle — bind Particles.UniqueID to an input pin, use as seed with a hash function
✓Custom module — wrap HLSL in a Scratch Pad module for reuse across emitters
✓Quaternion math — multiply quaternions manually (no built-in qmul)
✓Jitter — pre-bake random sphere offsets: RandomUnitFloat3(seed) * Radius
✓Loop modes — implement Once/Loop/PingPong via switch on age-based index
✓Early exit — guard empty arrays: if (NumPositions == 0) return;
Quaternion Multiply (Helper)

No built-in quaternion multiply in Niagara HLSL — use this:

float4 QuatMultiply(float4 Q1, float4 Q2)
{
  return float4(
    Q1.w*Q2.x + Q1.x*Q2.w + Q1.y*Q2.z - Q1.z*Q2.y,
    Q1.w*Q2.y - Q1.x*Q2.z + Q1.y*Q2.w + Q1.z*Q2.x,
    Q1.w*Q2.z + Q1.x*Q2.y - Q1.y*Q2.x + Q1.z*Q2.w,
    Q1.w*Q2.w - Q1.x*Q2.x - Q1.y*Q2.y - Q1.z*Q2.z
  );
}

// Usage: combine rotations
Particle.MeshOrientation =
  QuatMultiply(BaseRot, DeltaRot);
Debugging Tips
!Debug via Color — write intermediate values to Particle.Color to visualize
!Scale debugging — set Particle.Scale = float3(value, value, value) to see ranges
!NaN check — if particles disappear, check for 0/0 or sqrt(negative)
✓CPU fallback — stateless modules have dual CPU/GPU paths for offline rendering
✓Guard empty buffers — always check if (Count == 0) return; before buffer reads
⚡ In-Engine Optimization
Performance Tiers at a Glance
Tier 1 — Effectively Free
✓Combine emitters — 1 emitter × 1000 particles beats 10 emitters × 100 (fewer VM invokes / GPU dispatches)
✓Fixed Bounds — enable in System settings to prevent dynamic bounding box recomputation every frame
✓Always stop unused systems — looping systems that are invisible still consume GPU dispatch and CPU overhead
✓Effect Type asset — attach one to every system; enables budget-based culling with zero per-frame cost
Tier 2 — Cheap Wins
✓LOD via Scalability — configure Low / Medium / High / Epic / Cinematic quality levels in Effect Type
✓Distance culling — particles 2× farther away cost ~4× less fill rate; set aggressive cull distances
✓Texture atlasing — pack 4×4 SubUV frames into one atlas; drive with SubUV Animation module to cut draw calls
!Translucency budget <3ms — monitor with stat gpu; translucency is the first budget to watch
Tier 3 — Expensive
!Per-particle collision — each particle traces a scene query; keep counts low or use Scene Depth approximation
!Light emission — only available on CPU sims; each particle light adds a shadow-casting draw call overhead
!Many dynamic material params — per-emitter material parameter updates flush batching
!Full-screen translucent sprites — overdraw × material cost multiplies; minimize screen coverage
Tier 4 — Very Expensive
✗Unbounded GPU sims >1M particles — without fixed bounds, culling is unreliable and memory grows unbounded
✗Translucent overdraw >5× deep — the same pixel drawn 5–10 times = 5–10× material cost; use cutout masks instead
✗>10 active emitters per actor — each emitter is a separate compute dispatch; batch aggressively
✗Complex simulation on mobile — GPU sim is ES3.1-limited; test on lowest target device early
GPU vs CPU Simulation
When to Use GPU vs CPU
Decision FactorCPU SimulationGPU Simulation
Particle count sweet spot1 – 100100+ (thousands to millions)
Collision & gameplay eventsYes — full scene query supportNo — compute only, no callbacks
Light emissionYesNo
Module feature setFull — all Niagara modulesLimited — compute-compatible only
Texture samplingVia Data InterfaceVia Texture Sample DI only
Per-frame CPU thread costScales with particle countNear-zero (async GPU dispatch)
Memory pressureSystem RAMVRAM — watch on mobile/consoles
Offline / deterministic renderYes — CPU path availableGPU path only (non-deterministic)
Optimization Rules
DO — Emitter & System
✓Enable Fixed Bounds in System settings — prevents GPU particles from disappearing when emitter actor is off-screen
✓Batch emitters — combine emitters that share the same material into one emitter with SubUV variation
✓Atlas textures — 4×4 SubUV atlas = 16 visual variations, 1 draw call
✓Effect Type asset — define per-quality budgets once and reuse across all systems in the project
✓Deactivate off-screen systems — use Significance Handler to pause systems beyond a set distance
AVOID — Emitter & Render
✗Never-ending loops without culling — idle GPU dispatches drain budget even when nothing is visible
✗Full-screen translucent quads — overdraw × material cost is your largest GPU risk
✗Dynamic bounding boxes — force GPU to re-derive bounds every frame; switch to Fixed Bounds for stable sims
!Translucency >3ms total — monitor with stat gpu or GPU Insights (UE 5.4+); translucency is additive across all effects
!No Significance Handler without Effect Type — budget overruns go unmanaged; add Effect Type first
Overdraw Reduction
✓Cutout / alpha erosion masks — replaces full rectangular sprite with shaped cutout, drastically reducing covered pixels
✓Reduce particle size — smaller screen footprint = less fill; pair with higher emitter count if needed
✓Distance-based transparency fade — fade particles out before they're too small to be meaningful
!Avoid Lit translucency on small particles — Unlit is significantly cheaper; reserve Lit for hero VFX only
Scalability & Effect Types
Effect Type Asset — Budget Controls

The Effect Type is a global scalability asset — create one, attach it to every Niagara System via System settings, then configure these per-quality-level budgets:

Max Global Budget UsageCull entire systems once the total Niagara ms budget across the scene is exceeded — the most important setting to set
Max Distance ScaleShrinks cull distance proportionally as the budget fills — near-full budget = more aggressive distance culling automatically
Max Instance Count ScaleGlobally scales down simultaneous particle system instances across all systems that share this Effect Type
Max System Instance CountPer-system instance cap — prevents any one effect from monopolising the budget at the expense of others
Scalability levelsLow / Medium / High / Epic / Cinematic — configure per-level behaviour in Project Settings → Niagara
Significance Handler
✓Significance Handler determines which systems stay active when over budget — closest / most visible effects survive
✓Assign a Significance Handler asset in the Effect Type — default handler sorts by distance
✓Custom handlers can weight by screen size, gameplay importance, or camera proximity
!Without an Effect Type, the Significance Handler has no budget thresholds to react to — both must be configured
Profiling Tools
stat gpuConsole command — shows GPU ms per pass; watch the Translucency and ParticleSimulation rows specifically
GPU Insights (UE 5.4+)Visual GPU profiler inside Unreal Insights — shows per-system and per-material GPU time on a timeline
Niagara DebuggerWindow → Niagara Debugger — live particle counts, active systems, budget usage per system while in PIE
viewmode shadercomplexityViewport display mode — visualizes per-pixel instruction cost; target green on all particle materials
r.Niagara.GPUCmdBufSafety 1Enables extra GPU safety checks in development builds — disable for shipping builds
